original_abstract: Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors such as BaTiO<sub>3</sub> support a remnant polarization after the application of an electric field that can promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Here, we demonstrate FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation with barium titanate nanocrystals for the first time. Nanocrystals of the ferroelectric tetragonal structure type were obtained by a hydrothermal synthesis from TiO<sub>2</sub> and barium hydroxide in 63% yield. BaTiO<sub>3</sub> nanocrystal films on tantalum substrates exhibit water oxidation photocurrents of 0.141 mA cm<sup>-2</sup> at 1.23 V RHE under UV light (60 mW cm<sup>-2</sup>) illumination. Electric polarization at 52.8 kV cm<sup>-1</sup> normal to the film plane increases the photocurrent by a factor of 2 or decreases it by a factor of 3.5, depending on the field polarity. It also shifts the onset potential by -0.15 or +0.09 V and it modifies the surface photovoltage signal. Lastly, exposure to an electric field increases the H<sub>2</sub> evolution rate of Pt/BaTiO<sub>3</sub> by a factor of ∼1.5, and it raises the selectivity of photodeposition of silver onto the (001) facets of the nanocrystal. All FE enhancements can be removed by heating samples above the Curie temperature of BaTiO<sub>3</sub>. These findings can be explained by FE dipole-induced changes to the potential drop across the space charge layer of the material. The ability to use the ferroelectric effect to enhance hydrogen evolution and water oxidation is of potential interest for the development of improved solar energy for fuel conversion systems.
humanized_abstract: Cultured BaTiO<sub>3</sub> nanocrystals enable the photocatalytic evolution of hydrogen and the photoelectrochemical oxidation of water Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors such as BaTiO<sub>3</sub> have a permanent polarization (remnant polarization) after the application of an electric field and which can also facilitate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. To our knowledge this is the first report of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation in any material. Nanocrystals of the ferroelectric tetragonal structure type were obtained in 63% yield from a hydrothermal synthesis of mixtures of aqueous solutions of TiO<sub>2</sub> and barium hydroxide. Photosponsored water oxidation in films of the nanocrystals deposited on tantalum showed a photocurrent of 0.141 mA cm<sup>-2</sup> at 1.23 V RHE under UV light (60 mW cm<sup>-2</sup>) which could be increased by an application of an electric polarization (field) of 52.8 kV cm<sup>-1</sup> normal to the substrate plane by a factor of 2 or decreased by a factor of 3.5 in different polarities and was also shifted by -0.15 or +0.09 V. In addition the surface photovoltage signal was also modified. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over Pt loaded in BaTiO<sub>3</sub> was also found to be increased by an applied electric polarization by a factor of ∼1.5 and which had a higher selectivity for photodeposition of silver at the (001) facets of the nanocrystals. The FE enhancements are eliminated upon heating the sample above the Curie temperature of BaTiO<sub>3</sub> and are therefore reversible. The FE dipole-induced enhancements in photosponsored water oxidation and hydrogen evolution appear to be a result of a modification of the potential drop across the space charge layer of the semiconductor that increases the rate of water oxidation and/or the rate of the oxidation of the reduced photocatalyst (the hydrogen in the case of hydrogen evolution). The demonstration that the FE effect can be used to enhance the photocatalytic evolution of hydrogen and the photoelectrochemical oxidation of water is highly interesting and may have the potential to be useful in the development of methods to enhance solar energy conversion for efficient production of fuels from water.
